summary: A polynomial or rational inequality asks for the set of x-values that make one side larger (or smaller) than the other. The answer is a range — or a union of ranges — written with intervals.
method: Move everything to one side so the other side is 0.
Find the boundary values: the zeros of the numerator and the zeros of the denominator.
These boundaries split the number line into intervals.
Test one point from each interval to see whether the inequality is true there.
Combine the intervals that work, using a round bracket for a strict < or > and a square bracket for ≤ or ≥ (a value that makes a denominator zero is always excluded).

example.title: Solve a quadratic inequality
steps: The boundaries are the roots of x² − 4 = 0, namely x = −2 and x = 2.
They split the line into (−∞, −2), (−2, 2) and (2, ∞).
The strict > excludes the boundaries themselves.
